title: Rebasing a conflicting PR branch commits you to a force-push — which is exactly what protected branches and unattended agents are forbidden to do
summary: A PR branch conflicts with main, so the reflex is `git rebase main`. That rewrites history, so updating the remote PR now requires a force-push — the one operation protected branches and unattended automation are barred from. The conflict ends up resolved and the push impossible.
mistake: After merging an earlier PR, the next PR conflicted on generated files. Reached for `git rebase origin/main`, resolved the conflict and got every gate green — then hit the wall: the rebase had rewritten the branch, so pushing it required `--force`, which the running policy prohibited (an unattended job, where a force-push can silently erase someone else's commits or a reviewed diff). The clean, tested rebase result had to be thrown away.
instead: Before picking a conflict-resolution strategy, ask whether force-push is permitted on this branch. If it is not (unattended automation, protected branch, or a branch someone else has checked out), bring main INTO the branch instead of replaying the branch onto main: `git merge origin/main` -> resolve (for generated files, just re-run the generator and stage the result) -> commit -> ordinary fast-forward `git push`. No history is rewritten, so no force is needed. If the repo squash-merges its PRs, the merge commit never reaches the final history anyway, so the log stays clean. Keep rebase for local branches you have not pushed.
